commentBody:
Interesting grid, very interesting fill, generally gentle clues, not too much misdirection. Thanks for the fun, Sam.

(I liked both themelesses this week; I found this one easier.)
Happy weekend! This felt like a fairly straightforward and quick solve. Fun though.
Fun one. Had me scared for a bit when not much was filling in.

Using an “S” instead of a “Z” in STYLIZE/REZ caused me way too much trouble.  Took me a long time to find my mistake!  

I’ve really enjoyed this entire week’s puzzles.  The challenge really amped up relative to recent weeks.

That was good fun, and I never got stuck!

Sam Ezersky has a idiosyncratic taste for cute punny clues and his puzzles are always fun (though I cannot recall a previous Saturday by him). And this puzzle has a stack of three long answers crossing a big thick vertical slab of white space! Quite the puzzl-o-rama.


(But I *like* to get stuck on Saturday puzzles. Sort of how I like the effect of many Scoville units on my psyche, banging my head against a crossword puzzle seems to leave me with intellectual endorphin that courses through my bloodstream raising spirits along the way. I hope Sam's next Saturday offering will be an appropriately killer challenge without sacrificing his signature punniness.)
I for one appreciated this funky grid. Completely different flow and it looked cool to boot.

I often have mixed feelings about Sam Ezersky's constructions but today was absolutely brilliant with some superb misdirects. Starting last Sunday, this has been one of the more challenging (and fun) weeks in recent memory and though it's unlikely, I'd like to think it's based on all the 'too easy for an end of week puzzle, where are the difficult puzzles like the old days' feedback we've been giving on this forum over the past several months.
For a while I was wondering if Drunk Twerking was a fad I missed

I was about to complain about the Wordplay picture but a quick search softened my attitude.  Copilot returned a multi-paragraph response that ended with the following:

"In summary:
Swans do often mate for life, especially in species like Mute Swans, Trumpeter Swans, and Tundra Swans. But it’s not universal — some swans will replace a mate if breeding fails or if the bond breaks, and occasional promiscuity occurs. So while the “eternal love” image is largely true for many swans, it’s not a guarantee for all."

Tonight, tonight,
It’s Sam Ezersky, right?
It might befog, but it won’t be cliche. 
Tonight, tonight,
We’ll all feel heard tonight,
Eres mía, on the rez, anyway!

Today, all day my knee caps had a feeling,
Some errata could happen,
But I know “mice” is right!
Big cheese with bread,
And through the grid we sped,
Tonight!

When I see Sam Ezersky’s name on a puzzle, I hesitate a moment while in my mind I hear the first few notes of the theme music from the movie “Jaws”. (This also happens whenever someone references the Art Heist puzzle in these comments.) Once I get past that initial fear I always enjoy the solve, and today was no exception. Thanks, Sam!

Great ending to a great week; seeing Sam’s byline on a puzzle is sure to inspire equal parts terror and anticipation in followers of his work. This was certainly not his most torturous, but not his easiest either; his last outing, if I recall, was downright gentle in comparison. 
A cursory look at the top half of the grid begged me to head to the bottom, which was good advice; Spider-Man trivia gave me DOCOCK, the D got me FONDA and I finally had a foothold. Despite that the top half still came only with difficulty, and I was starting to despair of the NE until I had the PR brainwave, and gave up ESS for BOW once I got BOGIE and took a stab at the B for BAG.
I don’t envy Lewis picking top 5 clues for this week. There were several candidates yesterday, and surely SEEINGEYEDOG and INFLIGHTWIFI need to added, and possibly PRAGENCY, KNEECAPS, and GOLFTEES.
I didn’t even notice the weird grid design until I read the column—what a unique way to STYLIZE this puzzle.
VERYWELLTHEN, I had a blast with this puzzle and the entire week, I hope this bodes well for more fresh puzzles ahead. I finally FEELHEARD by editors about returning to a greater challenge.

Do I dare
To say, "I care!"
about what?
This!
A grid to fill
True love
A kiss

This puzzle was one of those that seemed impossible at first then opened up nicely.  It felt appropriately Saturday to me.  

@Mean Old Lady, @Sotto Voce, @Seward Parker and anyone else I missed:  I plan to join you at Cloud City Coffee on the corner of Roosevelt Way and NE 88th St. in Seattle on Sunday.  Is the plan to meet at about 1pm?  I will be wearing earrings that evoke a crossword grid (black-and-white checkerboard).  I’m looking forward to meeting all of you!  Safe travels, @Sotto Voce.

I, for one, am not too proud to say that that puzzle was yet another Chomolungman challenge for me.  I liked it a lot, but I did spend a lot of time simply staring and thinking.  There was a strong odor of bacon in the air.

I am also not too modest to say that I got it.  Finally.

It feels these days that if I am trying to recall something, a name, a place, if for even a millisecond I have a moment of doubt that I'll remember it, I'll certainly fail to remember it.  

I had trouble thinking of "Facebook" earlier when I was trying to unravel 14D.  Facebook.  The engram that is cemented in every human mind.

Yet, all in all, I'm getting along pretty good with my brain in my old age.  It's fraying at the edges, and it knows it, but I try not to make it ponder it too long.  

Another good thing about an aging brain--it's gullible enough to accept that I really do think I'm getting better looking with age.

Gray is a very underrated color.

"Not only do I get water from this deep excavation in the ground - I get fruit too!"
"Berry well then."

(I can dig that.)

Am I the only one who saw IO MOTH and thought this might somehow be themed, since the grid kind of looks like a sideways moth? And, of course, Sam edits the Spelling Bee, so maybe it’s a bee instead. Then, to top it all off, Caitlin’s first paragraph uses the phrase “Time flies.” So maybe it’s a fly? Or maybe it’s late and I’m just seeing things.
"BEFOG" caused an audible groan from me.
Great Friday night treat, Sam!  Loved the mini, too.

I take back anything bad I may have said about Sam Ezersky. That was fun and original. 

Would love to know how many times Will Shortz had to reject the clue for 52 A.

Very nice Saturday puzzle. Thought I had absolutely no hope for the first 20 minutes or so, then the long entries started falling into place and it gradually rolled over and let me tickle its tummy. Finished almost exactly on my Saturday average time in the end.
As others have commented, this has been a really good week of crosswords from the NYT.
Thanks Sam, and I'm looking forward to a beauty of a Sunday!

Briefly: Ditto my Friday comment. Really challenging and well-constructed puzzle. Thank you! 

I got back home after watching the Milwaukee Brewers get drugged by their ace pitcher. I had to hit the Saturday puzzle though. I was well rewarded. I felt like I was doing a puzzle from 10 years ago. And, just like yesterday, that wonderful feeling of wondering how I was ever going to finish it, over and over again.
The grid is really cool. Turned 90 degrees it looks like the first answer, iomoth.

Must say, I GAT right BEFOGged by some of these solves. It seemed that someone was trying to BOGEY me. Nevertheless, I ATEOF the platter before me and am now off to ponder DOCOCK's tentacles at an OTB. Excellent puzzle!
great puzzle, Sam! it's like you all were sitting on these to debut post-ACPT... good run lately
Loved this one! Very thoughtful cluing.

Per constructor notes. 
“I recognize this effort might ultimately land in polarizing fashion, but if nothing else, I hope it makes for a refreshing change of pace!”

Yes Sam. You changed my solving pace to almost 1 hour. Instead of 30 minutes. Refreshing!

I’m glad I don’t see constructor names until after I solve. Sam usually destroys me. Today was no different.  But at least a gold.  I’m glad it’s Friday night, so I had more time to savor the challenge. 

The grid was definitely unique. I wonder if a random constructor submitted this grid, would it get insistent dismissed?  Hey. Sometimes it held to have ‘connections’, err I mean “spelling bee”

This has been a fantastic stretch of puzzles. Sunday kicked off one of the best weeks I can remember.

I thought I was gonna get Ezerskied tonight but, miraculously, it all filled in. And I loved it.

This was so convoluted and tricky that when one of my early fills like ANKLES stood their ground I had to breathe into a paper bag (not a MANBAG) to keep from fainting.  I mean, the thrills just kept on coming, but unfortunately, so did the clumsy flubs, like sap and specify, and totally missing the golf shot.

What a trip this was, and how I loved it.  It's what a pro has to offer and what we have the extraordinary luck to enjoy.  Thank you, Sam.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
My hat is off to this setter whose grids typically rub me not the right way.

Pretty...
Pretty...
Pretty good, Sir.

I liked an Ezersky!

It’s a peninsula not a land 

OTB remains a mystery 

BOGEY was a favourite. 

Excellent Saturday. Thanks

Emus seem hungry today.
The mini was brilliant today.
I braced myself for the worst seeing Mr Sam E's byline, but I could fill in a decent amount before getting truly stuck. Using more normal clues definitely helped.
Wow, how perfect, how enjoyable, how fun! I dig it! What a great way to start a relaxing Saturday morning!

Hmmm…. A bit easier than I would’ve liked for a Saturday…. Yesterday’s was a lot harder. 
I think I sorta enjoyed it… 
Catch 22 references two consecutive Saturdays! Better start re reading in anticipation of next week…
2 potential corrections: (1) They aren't "mice"'; they are "mouses." (2) Internet denizens are also called "netizens."

It is very fitting that Ankle was my foothold for which I got started.   Without it, I don't think I ever would have gotten off the ground.    A very fun puzzle that had my mind spinning to come up with the correct answers.    Confidently putting on Ette instead of Enne really stifled my progress for quite a while, as did putting in Unite instead of Unify.... But eventually I determined those errors and was able to continue on and eventually get my gold star.
I have to imagine a lot of us got 52 across in there early one, and were doing double takes every time we came back to that corner

I didn’t realize until after the fact that this was an Ezersky puzzle.  Now it all makes sense. This one had more mines than the strait of Hormuz, but I struggled most in the NE corner.  CRIMEA eluded me for a long time because I thought land had to mean country.  Get one over on seemed unfathomable, and I shot  myself in the foot by plopping  ess in for curved shape instead of BOW.  It came together at long last when I finally came up with DEARANN.  I loved the clue “Helpful pointer, say?” for SEEINGEYEDOG and I loved the unbalanced grid with its long downs of varying lengths.  I found this a most worthy Saturday morning challenge.

When I saw the byline, I tensed, knowing it would be a toughie. But I said out loud, “This is going to be a good one!” I was not disappointed.

Quadruple-play for Sam today: great crossword, clever mini, Spelling Bee of course, and let´s not forget Letter Boxed!

Ahh, a Sam Ezersky Saturday.  After yesterday's breezy solve, this one made me work for it.

There wasn't any particular sticking point, just a few unknowns scattered throughout along with some exceptional cluing for things I know.

[Get one over on] almost got one over on me.  What a perfect clue.  I learned what a BOGEY is in another crossword puzzle long ago, but I wasn't making that connection.  Even when it couldn't be anything else and I had to write it in, it made no sense until I went over everything in that area one more time and it hit me.
This was a good workout. Had to have one little nudge, but that helped things fall into place. A classic start to Saturday!
Lovely puzzle. One that gives nothing on the first try but gradually opens up. Ms FONDA held me up a bit in the end.
Having trouble accessing comments in NYT wordplay app - I need to go to the web version, has anyone had issues with this and can recommend solutions? (using Android version)

What you can count on in an Ezerski:
•	Junk-be-gone grid.  Cleanroom quality in the box.
•	Something novel.  Today it’s the up-down mirror symmetry, the least used symmetry -- only six times -- in the history of the Times crossword.
•	Whole lotta debut answers.  Ten today, including many worthy additions to the Times oeuvre: ADD TIP, I FEEL HEARD, IN-FLIGHT WIFI, PENALTY KICK, SEEING EYE DOG, and my favorite – VERYY WELL THEN.
•	Something spectacular.  Today it’s those crossing middle-grid three-stacks, all six answers not only colorful, but NYT answer debuts!  “Wow!” isn’t strong enough…

All of this, of course, flies out the window, if the solve isn’t pleasurable when all is said and done.  For me, it certainly was.  I kept having “Oh, that’s good!”  moments.  such as the inner somersault I did when I understood [Get one over on] for BOGEY.

Pure quality from start to finish, as usual, Sam.  You exemplify the art and science of making crosswords.  Thank you!

The confluence of 
ERES MÍA
GAT
REZ
STYLIZE
was a TIL moment times 4.

Fun puzzle.

Ooooooh too hard! So many words I hadn't heard of today. At least I knew PENALTY KICK.

Usually US English is shorter and snappier than UK, but we have 'guide dog' for SEEING EYE DOG. I assume ANN was an agony aunt? Is MID CITY really used? And is 'downtown' actually in the middle of cities? I'm glad many people enjoyed it though.

This may be my favorite puzzle from my favorite constructor. The grid was intriguing, the fill was fresh, the clues were just puzzling enough, and the frustration level was just enough to make me feel satisfied upon solving. Fantastic!!

For some reason I stopped using it, but as I filled in REs I was thinking that I would want the pencil option. (Is it still there?)

Anyway, it was a struggle to get there, but replacing the S with the correct Z was what rescued me from "Not quite" land.

Very challenging, yet enjoyable.

Fun puzzle. It would have been a very smooth solve for me if any of the following conditions had held:

a) I had remembered Cathcart from Catch 22

b) I had been much quicker to realize that corporal isn’t the only military rank with an abbreviation that matches “C_L”

c) I did *not* despise superhero movies

Alas, I was instantly committed to CPL and thought DOCpCK looked as legit as any other possibility — and even with the aid of hindsight, I can’t say that DOCOCK looks any better.

Aside from that, I thought it was pretty gentle for an Ezersky Saturday, but still a very nice grid with minimal filler/crosswordese.

Good lord, an Ezersky grid that didn’t completely melt my brain! Are you mellowing with age sir?
Having girded my loins (not easy with a bad back) I found I was a fair way into this before my first hold up. My answers were pretty scattergun to start; I put in I CARE, then removed it at least twice as I couldn’t imagine an insect with an IO start. I got FEEDBACK but couldn’t work out the beginning of that answer for ages. Like a lot of the fill, once I did get it, it seemed obvious, but not when staring at blank space.
I see @Jane Wheelaghan pointed out the Guide Dog/SEEING EYE DOG UK/US differential. I had DOG for so long, but couldn’t figure out the rest. And so it went on, the grid filling in slowly but surely.

I’m cross with myself I mixed hate with Iago’s RAGE. I’m meant to be a Shakespearean BA for goodness sake! 
Fave clue of the day, Book Boo Boos.
Isn’t it all indigenous land and the REZ is just what we allowed them to have back after stealing it?

Another wonderful, chewy puzzle.

After having sent a few peppery emails to the editors seeking crunchier puzzles - and as I said to my bosses after giving them my MIDYEARFEEDBACK - I feel tasted.

Easiest Ezersky puzzle ever ... for me. Still took 22 minutes. You know it's your day when you can immediately enter IOMOTH as 1A. Flew through this one---only unknown today was DOCOCK.

I think RES/STYLISE should also be considered correct---at least for any solver where British English is standard.

"Down-low joints" for ANKLES was one of several very clever clues today.

Well, typical tough Saturday for me. Ten debut answers, and at least three of them (MIDYEARFEEDBACK, INFLIGHTWIFI, DRUNKTWEETING) completely unfamiliar terms. Must confess that I cheated a bit to get through this one. No big deal. And... puzzle find today - a Saturday from July 21, 2018 by Jason Flinn. Two quadruple stacks of 15 letter answers at the top and bottom of the puzzle (and another 15 across the middle). Don't recall ever seeing that before. And 5 of those answers appearing for the first and only time, though none of them were terribly unfamiliar. Some examples: STRINGORCHESTRA ORDEREDALACARTE GOESOVERITAGAIN SUITEDONESNEEDS REASONTOBELIEVE Pretty amazing. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=7/21/2018&g=58&d=A ....
One look at the author's name and I wasn't sure I would be able to complete this. Very solvable with strident effort.
Well, now I can't get that song from "Bye, Bye Birdie" out of my head. "Kids, I don't know what's wrong with these kids today. Kids, who can unterstand anything they say." etc

[ Ruh-roh. 
An Ezersky grid
with top/bottom symmetry…
and a big black square on one side..?

On a Saturday.

Is it supposed to be an emoticon?

Have I had enough caffeine for this puppy?

Welp, here goes nothin…]


I loved it.
Crunchy, but oh-so doable.
Clever, crafty, cute and conquerable.


And,  because exactly *zero* folks 
care or even remember, 

it has been 1,000 days since the
tip/tap
tim/tam
tic/tac
natick that ended my streak.

In my opinion, 
the only *true*  natick I’ve ever seen.


( I can’t believe anybody has read this far. )

Have a lovely weekend all!

Another tough (and wonderful) solving experience for this guy.  The weekend has proved to be a good, ol' fashioned ONE-TWO punch so far.

Slow and steady progress was my experience, slowed down to a crawl in both the NE and SE by mistakes that had me repeatedly dope-slapping myself.  Like Caitlin, it took me forever to see the wordplay in "Got one over on", and I thought I was clever when I put in YEET for "Give a hoot?"  Had CEO for the longest time until I paid attention to the "bread" in the clue.

[As a small aside, I so wanted 28-Across to be THAT'S THAT, THEN.  It would have gone so WELL with THIS at 5-Down.]

As a sign of my age, I thought NEOPETs were pre-internet creatures.  Does anyone else remember those digital pets kids had on their keychains that needed to be tended to every so often?  I looked them up later, and found that I was thinking of "Tamagotchis".  Anyone care to check if that animal has ever found its way into a crossword?

Loved the grid, but spent too much time sweating the answers to fully appreciate it during the solve.

Thanks for the hard work, Sam Ezersky!

Yesss, a real Saturday!

Lots of fun stuff in here: DRUNK TWEETING, MAN BAG, DOC OCK and his LEGGS. I was impressed with myself for remembering the Treaty of GHENT.

Once I got enough fill from you all, I was able to complete it well. 

Saturday’s nerd fest left me behind, but that’s okay.

Good chewy Saturday puzzle! Thanks, Sam.

Caitlin, love your photo pick to illustrate DRUNK TWEETING.
Loved it!
Nice tough Saturday. If it weren’t for the well known politician I might still be sweating this one. I also like the unusual symmetry.

I was quite surprised to see MIDyearreview and MIDcity in the same puzzle. It seems like the kind of (non-theme) repetition that would ordinarily be frowned upon — regardless of the grid's other virtues. I wonder if the author or editor cares to offer insight!

If a previous commenter has made this observation, I apologize for belaboring the point.
This Sam puzzle required sleeping on twice, but when I finally woke up so many things that had seemed ungettable became clear. And all was forgiven when I solved SEEING EYE DOG.

Chewy Saturday puzzle. I was pretty much befogged. I don't often pay via app, so I kept trying to squeeze in "add item." Of course, I do see the ubiquitous "add tip" at the register (something us older generation folks still use)
Normally I think Sam Ezersky puzzles are too smart for their own good but this one was surprisingly not that bad

Saw Sam's byline and swallowed hard. This was fun to solve. TADA! Finished on BOGEY which I thought was a very clever clue.
Funny how many letters the Treaties of Ghent and Trent share but I couldn't remember which one it was. Had Ette before ENNE

I had never heard of an iomoth, so I needed a reveal on the top left.  I should have been able to get it with crosses, but just couldn't for the life of me figure out what a tired expression was, even when I had -LICHE. I kept trying to think of a facial expression.  Even *after* the reveal, I was like, "what's a CLICHE?!" (pronounced like YEESH). My brain was definitely BEFOGGED for a moment there.  😂😂😂

It took me almost an hour over half the day but I managed to complete this without outside help. I found the puzzle extremely hard but ultimately solvable. I'm not sure if I liked it - the effort was a bit much for me, and I generally prefer easy wins to hard-won ones 🤣

Sam, you really got one over on me with that clue for BOGEY...and I play golf. And not very well, so I get plenty of those.

And since Becky hasn't stopped by yet, come on you Gunners!
I would be interested to know how many of Ezersky's submitted clues were changed by Will Shortz.
Talk about not being on the same wave length. I persevered but it took me twice as long as normal. I'm tired now.

I had Latvia and leave before CRIMEA and CEASE. (No. I don't know geography - it just fit.)
Proud that once I had PR, I realized what kind of "image" we were going for. 
Had to flyspeck for the last letter. which was to change the "s" to a Z in REZ. I thought it was a slightly cringey clue. But what do I know.

This would make a great entry for some professional crossword competition. 

For those of us who do crosswords for enjoyment, for leisure, for an escape from the difficult realities of daily life, this is cruel torment. 

I ran through the clues and had to check the author. Ah, of course. 

In sticking with my long-held oath to boycott any puzzles by Mr. Ezersky, I hit reveal and got on with my day. 

Please don’t publish any more of his puzzles. We nonprofessional solvers deserve better.

Thank you, Sam,

Fiendish themeless puzzle.  Took 50% longer than my average Saturday time but fell into place with perseverance.  

‘Twas a worthy challenge!

Fortunately I didn't notice the Sam Ezersky byline until after I had solved it, or I would have gone in with more than usual trepidation.  It seemed hard as I gradually chipped away, but ended up under my Saturday average somehow.  Seemed like a perfect Saturday difficulty to me.  One of the last to fall was BOGEY, which just had to be right due to BEFOG -  but I didn't get it until I read the column - what a fun clue!   Thank you, Sam!
Tough for me but very satisfying

I had to look at the answer key as I had BOD crossing with ANYDAY. They both fit perfectly in my mind.

I found it incredibly tough, but hey, it's a Saturday!
Good stuff!
That Welsh barmaid apparently not familiar with the story of Leda and the swan.

So.....Might this be entitled "Sam's Revenge"??  All those complaints about the Word List of Spelling Bee (which still rejects NINON, I cannot help noting.)....  Is this a form of PENALTY KICK delivered to the OLDER GENERATION as we FADE away?

I had to DEFOG, then REFOG, and I was still BEFOG-GED.

Hey, Sam!  Can you imagine someone so OLD that her first hosiery was a pair of nylons with seams up the backs of the LEGs? Not to mention that they were held up with a garter belt!  And those early panty-hose....raise your hand, fellow-sufferers, if you had to make a "hiker" out of some elastic from the sewing kit.

I don't quite understand the "past tense" clue for 14D; does Sam think people have reformed?  Or is a TWEET no long a 'thing'?  Please answer quickly, as I may soon be so old I no longer remember how to read!
What a fun workout!
Excellent Saturday morning challenge for me. Took a while, but satisfied to earn my gold.

I knew of the creature in 1A, but I didn’t know what it was called. Another wrinkle was that 2D could have started with an E although I figured that was pretty unlikely.

I doubt it’ll ever happen to me again: solving the entire puzzle except for the #1 square!
A puzzle very worthy of a Saturday in my opinion. Took 15 minutes longer than my average. I was stuck with “RES” and “SYLISE” for a long time and had to reveal the one wrong letter. Oof.

I felt that calling the CRIMEA a land is pussyfooting. If the NYT games team doesn’t want to enter into geopolitics peninsula is available in the dictionary. 

An excellent Saturday. It took some time for the top of the puzzle to come together. 

BOGEY was a favourite and PRAGENCY was cute.

Darn it, I thought I might get a gold star today; I looked and looked and looked for what I had wrong… Alas, not being American has foiled me once more! I had an s instead of Z at the intersection of REs and STYLIsE - but had to ask the app to check my puzzle to find that error, so no gold star for me this week.

Sam, I really liked the puzzle. A real brain stretcher for me. I just need to tell you that Spelling Bee is my favorite! Thanks for all you do to make it so good. Also, sorry for all the stupid comments about which particular word you left out, even after you’ve explained it many, many times. Some people act like jerks; they can’t help it.

It took me almost an hour over half the day but I managed to complete this without outside help. I found the puzzle extremely hard but ultimately solvable. I'm not sure if I liked it - the effort was a bit much for me, and I generally prefer easy wins to hard-won ones 🤣

DO COCK looks intriguing.

This was tough, and I had to rely on a few clues from Caitlin's Wordplay column, but I got there in the end (and enjoyed it)! I read "land" as "country" in 1A and had RUSSIA for a while. RES vs. REZ hung me up as well.

On the other hand, I did manage to get 2D & 16D pretty quickly. I suppose it pays to be a cranky old soccer fan? 😄
Wow, that was crunchy for sure! Somehow got it done in an hour with no hints or lookups, but boy was it tough sledding!
Crunchy and satisfying, just the way I like my Saturday crosswords. Please keep 'em coming.

For 1A [Showy insect with dark eyespots on its wings], I started with "hornet." I was really thinking of "spotted lanternfly" which is a newly invasive species here that you're supposed to crush with your shoe. It has spots, doesn't it? 

For all you sports fans here, I'd like to mourn that the NFL Draft caused Jeopardy to be rescheduled last night. I missed it as it aired at 3:00 am. FWIT, Jamie Jeopardy pulled out another runaway win with an enormous lead. The final Jeopardy was about Bloomsday, something for James Joyce Ulysses fans. Jamie got it wrong; another contestant got it right, but Jamie couldn't be beat.

Time for me to be that guy again. 24D is messy. West Side Story has had two film and multiple stage versions. Song order has varied. "Tonight" is a duet between Tony and Maria; it is the show's version of the balcony scene from Romeo and Juliet, and occurs midway through Act I. The song that (usually? always?) precedes the Rumble is almost always called either Tonight Quintet or simply Quintet. here's an epic mash-up of the two film versions. Andrzej, you may be excused. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q7RvwD1kicE
I needed a couple of lookups but thoroughly enjoyed this puzzle. Well done Sam.

Not easy, but a little quicker than yesterday for me. Which fits with what xwstats has to say, gauging it as "Hard" rather than "Very Hard."

This has been a satisfying puzzle week. It looks as though most people have enjoyed it, so I hope a workable curve between Easy Monday and Crunchy Saturday has been found. 

I do not envy Shortz and the other editors their jobs!

And Sam, I like your quirky word selections for the Bee. I can never remember which acids you include and which you don't,  but even when I think I've gotten to know your list, many of the puzzles include some unfamiliar word that I get to learn. Usually discovered by furiously adding random prefixes and suffixes to word stems and being surprised that one of them is accepted. However, if I end up with arthritis in my fingers, it will be all your fault.

Loved it!

After getting stuck on PDFFILES yesterday like a marathon runner collapsing within sight of the finish line, it was nice to get through today’s challenge without consulting the blog post or outsourcing my issues to Rex Parker.

I did have a few speed bumps to deal with before getting the gold star - UTYLIZE before STYLIZE and INTS before INKS were the root of those hindrances.

OLDER GENERATION was the first long fill that stuck, and prior to that I had found little purchase anywhere else.

Cheers.

I wish I knew people in real life who did crosswords. I'm curious what drives people to love the Thursday to Saturday puzzles. I love the Sunday and Monday. I dread the themeless puzzles with long phrases. I never get the "aha" moment where things click into place. I just just try something and it might be right, wrong, or partially correct.
The Golf Tee clue had me stuck for a while, enjoyed figuring it out though.
Well, there you have it!! Rather tame for Sam, but still a great Saturday. Grazie!
Sam Ezersky's puzzle was full of bad clues and worse answers

I am almost never in tune with Sam's puzzles, but today was an easy Saturday for me.

My last letter filled in was PRAGENCY/RAGE, and I first asked myself "what the heck is pragency?" until I realized PR Agency, doh!
fabulous! so much fizzy misdirection and meaty fill. "scintillating" is the word!

We've watched all 4 seasons of Dark Winds and absolutely love it. We were hooked from S1E1 by the beautiful scenery and taken in by Joe Leaphorn. Although the term "rez' is waaaaaay overused, we look forward to another season.

The Fraulein assassin is scary as heck! She reminded me of the female version of Michael Meyers when she'd tilt her head with the gas mask on. {{shiver}}

My usual late puzzle find. A Wednesday from December 24, 2014 by Adam G. Perl. Three theme answers in the top half: CRYMEARIVER PARASAIL GOBETWEEN And then three more theme answers in the bottom half, exactly opposite each of those on the top: GOBITWEEN PARISALE CRIMEARIVER Here's that link; https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/24/2014&g=62&d=A ....

I got a good amount of the puzzle done on my work lunch break today, but was stuck on a few of the long entries. In particular, 32A: [Helpful pointer, say?]--I was pretty sure about the wordplay, and that "pointer" meant DOG, but I wracked my brain thinking, "What's the current, politically correct term for a seeing-eye dog? "support animal"? "assistance dog"? It was only on my bike-commute home I realized that the appropriate term for "seeing-eye dog" was "SEEING-EYE DOG"!

Holy shnikies!! This one solved as very hard for me. But I got it! Hip hip-hurum!! 

Each time I was successful at twisting my brain a little bit more to try to get into Sam's head space, I got a little burst of glee!

Or maybe it was my synapses bursting into the flames of hell, where they will spend a torturously unbearable eternity cursing my crossword hobby and refusal to look things up...  Har!! 

This was for sure, for me, very slow and wobbly, but fun!! 

I don't know what GAT or OTB are yet, so I have to look those up. I was so relieved to finish that I came straight here, after looking quickly to see what x word stats labeled this puzzle as. Phew! I can't believe I finished it!

I need to catch up on some work, but now I think I need a nap!
If anyone wants to try another Sam Saturday... https://www.nytimes.com/crosswords/game/daily/2019/02/23

Did not like this puzzle. Surprised Sam wrote it. I know we’re probably not on a first name basis, but after 1500+ puzzles, I feel like I'd recognize his work. Example Indian home, informally “Rez”. I think Res is informal. Rez would be slang. Just painful to get through. I hate long answers although i did answer them right after i had more of the small stuff done. Just ugh.
Thank you. This is the most fun I've had in many a Saturday morning. Excellent puzzle to be sure.
Awful puzzle. Horribles clues, worse answers. Very millennial coded and clearly made for one type of thinker.

Anyone else have sap for the gangster weapon? It's another name for a blackjack. When I was a kid and all the cops and robbers movies were common fare, every kid on the block could talk the talk whie we were chasing each other around the neighborhood.

I agree with all the commenters who noted and enjoyed the increased difficulty of the week's puzzles, especially the themeless. I found today's puzzle much easier than yesterday's which surprised me given the constructor. But, he is an editor also and so maybe was playing make-up after yesterday to keep the new paradigm on track. And for the experts, maybe absence makes the heart grow fonder, and maybe the occasional hard-week bone thrown to the puzzledogs will be the way they keep both old and new solvers engaged, with a kind of sinewave of comments switching from "too easy" to "too hard" depending on the week

For the longest time I had PHOTO to start 16A and ENCE for 23A. This made 23D _HUCKTWEETING. I really wanted to put a P in the blank. Alas, I couldn’t make that work :(  

But once that phrase entered my mind I find it very tantalizing, disturbing, and difficult to ignore all at the same time.

I found the Saturday fairly clued, but still particularly challenging. This was the first time in a long time I felt I might not solve it. I had so many fills left. I think I was not quite on the right wavelength today. But I stuck with it, getting OLDERGENERATION, SEEINGEYEDOG, and PENALTYKICK started the dominoes falling and finished slightly slower than my Saturday average. I quite enjoyed the challenge. Long story short, this made a 1000-day solve streak for me. Now onto the next 1000.
Ugh, tough one. I had to consult the answer key to get BOW , crossed with ANYWAY.  My answers were equally defensible. For curved shape, I had “BOD,” resulting in ANY DAY. 😊
Dear Caitlin, It was lovely to read the job you did on this puzzle's author, who richly deserves it.

I know I’m one of those people to grouse when I feel a Sam Ezersky crossword is not entirely baked, so I figure it’s only fair to give credit where credit is due: This was a delight Sam. The misdirects were clever and the fill quite satisfying. My first pass was quite sparse, until I took a chance on 32A, which was a helpful pointer indeed, and the rest soon fell into place. Nicely done!

I enjoyed it even though I found it harder than even yesterday: Friday’s. It was as Sam said, with emphasis on the middle 12-letter fills. Had no idea
about INFLIGHT WIFI or SEEING EYE DOG until almost the end. A lot of word checks but this was hardest in the middle for me :) Had Drunk Writing and thought “Oh well” but totally forgot about TWEETING, drunk or not!
Sam, this was one of my favorite puzzles ever. Was the fact that the grid design looks like an Io Moth (1 Across) intentional?
This one was full of clues that looked impossible, but that made total sense once solved.  Bravo!

Helpful pointer? Cute clue if it's referencing the dog breed, except pointers are not used for SEEING EYE DOGS. If it's just referring to a dog "pointing" someone in the right direction, it's yet another vague, slightly weird clue like KNEECAPS being sore spots for gardeners that leaves me thinking, "I guess that's it..."

I can never quite seem to jibe with Ezersky puzzles, but I do appreciate a dedicated constructor.

Rez as the answer to Indigenous land is wholly offensive and whitewashing. All land in the USA is indigenous land and rez’ are where we put them after we engaged in genocide. Pretty shocked at your ignorance.

@Barry Ancona

Progress!  We have Barry using the comparative form of "easy" now!
@Barry Ancona 🤣🤣 you’re 💪ing … it was not gentle … it was a very decent Saturday.

Ιασων,
On xwstats.com, the Saturday median solve time is more than two minutes faster than Friday's median solve time. I wrote "I found this one easier," not that it was an easy puzzle.
@David Johnson Same. Only had a couple filled in after my first pass and thought “uh oh”.
@Dean - That was my last correction to get the gold star.
@jennie Same.

@Nick
same

I agree, it's been a good week, and hopefully because our voices are being heard!

@Bill in Yokohama
Is sad. Because the masses will blame the grid or constructor when it’s “too easy.”

We all know it’s about the clueing. 
The editors could take a Monday grid and make it a Saturday, if they wanted. 

But to your point.  It’s been a great ‘throwback’ week
@CB Bottoms up!

@CB
I didn’t realize one could twerk when sober

@CB
it's never too late...
@CB To twerk, or not to twerk.................

@Cat Lady Margaret

😂😂😂

Brilliant!  Better than Bernstein!

That's right, Leonard, you know I love you, but the Cat Lade is on fie-are TONIGHT.

@Francis
Better than Sondheim, you mean.

@SP

Just had another look at DOCOCK. If there was ever an entry to laugh at as a DOOK, this is it!

@SP

Even after getting it PRAGENCY is almost impossible for me to parse.  I keep seeing "pregnancy".

@Francis
I actually went to Merriam Webster to lookup “pragency “ as Sam is such a wordsmith 😂

@Francis

I’m not a Spider-Man fan, so what I know about the franchise is basically what I’ve learned in crosswords. I saw the clue and had a few letters already but nothing came to mind. 

I never went back to it because the crosses filled it in, and now, looking at the answer, I was thinking, doo-cock?  Dough-cock?

Oh, right. I vaguely remember a DOC OCK from some superhero franchise. That must be it!

@Steve L
Thank you, I had no idea!

@Cindy Totally with you there!
(Only referring to the first paragraph...!)
@Cindy Wordplay comments friends meeting up in real life - what a great thought! Have fun. Wish I was in the right part of the country to join you.

@sotto voce

Hey, Sotto, long time no text.

If you feel a NY disturbance in the PNW force, it might be because we just got to Eugene (our first trip to the left coast) to watch our son run its marathon. Then on to tourist around SFO. It’s beautiful around here!

Hope all is well and enjoy your Xword meetup!

@Cindy
See you tomorrow! I'll probably be wearing a black-and-white checkered shirt.

@Cindy et al
Getting excited/nervous.
I will be wearing white hair.  Ha ha.  And glasses.
Possibly a Cat Person shirt.

Warren! Yay! It's wonderful to hear from you! You're being missed.

The only reason I haven't inquired on your whereabouts is that I remember you having had it with the emus and advising us that you would switch to lurking.

BTW, I also remember something you said before you left... whenever I get reccos, I know you're one of them, lol. You're too funny!

Welcome to the PNW. I'm so glad you're enjoying the beauty – and in sunny weather to boot. 

I wish we'd known you'd be here.  A Wordplay meet-up could hve been set up in Eugene or Portland. I would have been happy to organize it.

Is your son here just for the marathon or is he living here?
In case he's living here and you head back this way, please let me know in advance and I'll happily take the reins on a meet-up.
I'm at sottovocepnw@gmailcom

I hope you enjoy your stay and have a fantastic time here and in SF. 
Good luck to your son and all the best to you and your family!

(And, please,  do pop in at least once in while? Like I said, you're being missed.)

@Warren

Steve Pre, eh? I'm not a runner, not even a jogger, but yes I know who it is, though I didn't know he went by Pre.

That's very cool that you and your wife follow your son around wherever there's a marathon. Brilliant idea. I LOLed with the deadheads allusion.

Congrats on the daughter getting Maui-ed! 
And thank you for the contact info. I'll definitely let you guys know if I head that way.

Take good care, kind sir!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Francis

Look at you!

Just a few weeks ago you were in the depths of despair about the inevitable cognitive decline which afflicts even the sharpest oldsters and considering hanging up the old spikes here.

Huzzah!

@Matt

I don't remember that.  Maybe I'm worse off than I thought.
@Francis It’s hard to imagine you getting better looking with age when you’re already devastatingly handsome.

@Francis
And I couldn't figure out why the clue said "once," until I read Rex Parker's blog (before Wordplay). To me and most everyone else, it's still tweeting, even if I would never touch that app again.

@Francis
I hope there was more thah just the ODOR of that bacon

@Mike

This pails compared to some of your better puns, but maybe you were waiting to use it and it was on your bucket list.

@Mike
If you got pieces of bread from it, you could mine your mannas.

@Mike
You and your Helpers (Enablers, I should call them) just keep shoveling the......pun-ishment.

@Aaron

I saw a cute short play by David Ives once called TIME FLIES about a pair of May flies who fall in love but discover  that their life span is only a day long and they are halfway through it already. Talk about existentialism…

@Derek

I even had BEdOG, as if the cloudiness were a metaphor, and thinking this was a phrase akin to "a dog's life".

@Derek I’ve used BEFOG so frequently in Wordle - it is particularly handy when the second letter is an L - that I’ve *almost* begun to think of it as normal.  Almost. I’ve never used it nor have I heard it in speech. I wouldn’t have thought of it for Wordle, either, but I noticed the Wordle bot using it.

@Valerie

TIL something new/fun from the mini, glad I did it.

@Some guy in
I finished the puzzle and got DOCOCK from the crossings. I know nothing about Spider Man and the answer looked totally bizarre. I knew it was right, because everything else fit. But it just seems weird. Don't even know how to pronounce it — DO? DOC?

@Times Rita

It's short for Doctor Octopus, on account that the character is a mad scientist with four mechanical arms. DOC OCK. It rhymes.

@Times Rita This one came straight out of my 12-year-old brain, still hiding deep inside after all these years. Doctor Octopus, so it's Doc. He was a villain who had a monstrous prosthetic body with multiple arms.
@Jake G - Hoping the Miz can beat Keller tomorrow night when we go.  Otherwise, I will be starting the Sunday puzzle at the game.
@jennie well, they can’t do much worse. A hit and a walk last night.
@Some guy in interesting. I wonder if that was intentional.

@Weak

The early returns look to be favorable. This afternoon, you’ll see “Lousy puzzle. Too many obscure answers and vague clues. 1/10.”

@Striker

I agree.

@Matt

I would have liked to see a couple/few more “Pretty”s in that sequence, but I enjoyed the reference nonetheless.

@Matt

To take off from both I FEEL HEARD and West Side Story, “I Feel Pretty”…
@Ιασων OTB = Off Track Betting, they’re physical locations so that one can place a wager on thoroughbred horse racing without actually being at any of the racetracks in NY state.
@Ιασων SEEINGEYE DOGS.
@Anthony thanks

@Anthony

OTB hasn’t been a thing in NY since 2010. It cut into the tracks’ profitability and they didn’t like it. 

But now you have all kinds of sports betting on your phone. 

Progress???

Ιασων,
Re: "It's a peninsula"

Set my soul free"

@Barry Ancona fair comment. 

I was assuming NYT was avoiding a touchy subject and my view was that peninsula avoided the issue. Land is somewhat more nuanced involving the people living there.

Ιασων,
I agree. I knew the answer, but I did cringe at the clue.
@Ιασων The clue was not a land. It was land which Crimea certainly is.

@Ιασων
So Italy isn't a land? News to the Italians, I suspect.

@Alexia

And today’s- fantastic. Loved big cheese with bread

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight

They are MICE. Mouse’s is an atrocity.

In fact, mouses is such an atrocity that autocorrect changed it to the possessive mouse’s.

@Steve L

It's mousodes, anyway.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight
Mouses? Your kidding…right?
Nothing wrong with mouses, except it makes some cringe. https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/mouses

@Steve L

My comment was a side note. I'm sorry you didn't understand that

@Steve L
Hmph. I bet it doesn't even acknowledge the present singular of the verb "to mouse", which is what your cat does when you're asleep, if it's a mouser.

@Steph 

I don't have trouble accessing the comments, but if there are so many comments on one thread, See More Comments will usually not open for me. I have to go to the NYT website, open Games in the index,  open the Wordplay column, open comments and then I can access all the comments.
Plus a pair of lovely serendipities -- the symmetrical IO MOTH and FLOATS, and FEEL HEARD coming two days after FEEL SEEN.
The middle of many U.S. cities is called "downtown," but in the City of New York downtown is a direction or a state of mind. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z_m4Qb0iW-o
Conversely... https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hCuMWrfXG4E

@Jane Wheelaghan 
Oh I don’t know about US English being shorter and snappier. The clarity and brevity of Anglo-Saxon flat and lift sure beat our polysyllabic, Latinate apartment and elevator.

@Paul Turner 
And for that matter, your “agony aunt” is snappier than our “advice columnist.”  ANN refers to Ann Landers, one of the twin sisters who had a near monopoly on the syndicated newspaper agony aunt business in the U.S. for decades. The other wrote as Dear Abby.

@Jane Wheelaghan ANN Landers was the agony aunt (love that Britishism) in the newspaper I read growing up. If I remember correctly, she was Dear Abby's twin sister. Both were syndicated but Abby had a wider circulation.

@Jane Wheelaghan

I think “downtown” usually comes from going towards where the street numbers are lower—usually the first streets are in the older part of town and they go up from there. But often the city is built around it so it starts to sprawl in all directions, so downtown can be more in the center. The numbers mainly go up one way only, but then they name the streets the other direction something else. Unless there’s a natural barrier like a river or ocean. MIDCITY would be more specifically geographical. Not sure how many cities really have an area called “midcity” but it is often a commercial name attached to companies, “Midcity Car Wash”, something like that.

@Jane Wheelaghan
ANN Landers was a famous advice columnist syndicated in gazillions of newspapers for years. I believe she was the sister of Dear Abby, an even famouser advice columnist.
@lucky13 famouser 👍

@SP
Considering:
1) how many settlements began on bodies of water because of easy access and then developed from there, and
2) how often that shoreline or beach was topographically the low-lying point in the settlement, because otherwise flooding would make the adjacent lands useless,
I suspect that "downtown" has always begun as "down by the river/lake/sea."
Later developments may have changed the focus of urban activity but think of where the original "downtown" of any major city began.

@Jack McCullough

Pencil Mode is still there, at least on Android. (✏️ icon)

@Eman

Who is “we”?

@Anonymous

Who do you think?

commentBody:

@JohnWM

I was just wondering which constructor would be the first to have us feel smelled.

@ad absurdum
Who will dare to have us FEEL FELT?

@Xword Junkie s to Z for the win! Haven’t been an Ezersky fan, but I liked this one, maybe because it’s his “easiest”.  ERES mia the other unknown to me. Nice 12 letter stack and adjacent 15 letter downs!

@The X-Phile
look at a picture of one and you're unlikely to forget it.
@Marcia Fidler I'm only in a slightly better position, because "Tonight" from West Side Story is in mine.

@Marcia Fidler

The immortal Paul Lynde, the center Hollywood Square for so long. Now it’s stuck in my head!

Perfect in every way?

With kids today?"

Won't be just any night
Tonight there will be no
Morning light!"

@Tony & Maria
I started with videos of the Quintet and then segued to Dance at the Gym. It's exhausting. ;)
@CCNY is supposed to be the Io moth!

@CCNY
Can you believe I nevder noticed the giant box o' black until I read your Comment!??!
Is this legal?
Get your pitchforks and torches, Solvers!  To the barricades!

@The X-Phile

TAMAGOTCHI - once

TAMAGOTCHIS - once

.....

@Rich in Atlanta Oh, and that TAMAGOTCHI appearance. A Sunday from December 20, 2020 by Randolph Ross with the title "Toy Story." Some other answers in that one: BARBIEDOLL ETCHASKETCH TICKLEMEELMO MRPOTATOHEAD RUBIKSCUBE SILLYPUTTY CHATTYCATHY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=12/20/2020&g=24&d=A ....

@Jane Wheelaghan
I'm dizzy with imagining what could have incited such Draconian treatment. Do you have any notion of what triggered the emus?

@Katie 

GHENT was similarly clued in the Sunday, April 12 puzzle.  Not to be confused with the Council ot TrENT. :)

@Marcus

Not a constructor or editor, but the general rule is that it has to be the whole word. And even so, an answer can be repeated in its entirety in service of a theme (se Thursday’s puzzle).

And in this case the word is being used 
in combining form as part of a word (Mid-).

@Marcus

It might depend on editor or circumstance. I didn’t notice this one, but was a bit put off earlier this week by FLY crossing FLYING START. In contrast, I am in the processing of revising a puzzle for LA Times consideration, and had to basically redo the whole thing because I had INSIDE as part of a phrase and SIDEB as another clue. Not only that but I had another phrase starting with NO and ANO as another clue, which had to be revised because they said the LA Times always clues ANO as A NO (“that’s ____) instead of the Spanish year, and didn’t want NO twice. And that’s for a Sunday. So some editors can certainly be pickier about it.

@SP
Interwsting that in LA they are not willing to ignore the tilde.

@Steve L

I had MIDTOWN at first. MIDCIITY does seem to me like a bit of a made-up word, although I'm sure it's in use.

SP,
Any reason they won't clue it as the Portuguese year?

@Barry Ancona

Or the Spanish other thing?

@Steve L
I don’t know that’s just what they told me. It’s interesting because I do think they allow other words with a tilde, like senor, but for some reason ANO is a sticking point

@SP

You might try Kingdom Come Deliverance II if you like tainted grail. I'm enjoying it a lot, though combat at the beginning is brutally hard. Lots of mods, though, if you want to tweak things. It's very immersive. It's also based on places that exist and some of the characters and events existed/happened.

@Wyra

I tried KCD II and didn't like it, at all. The lack of save-on-demand combined with a steep learning curve was too much for me. Also, much of what my character was expected to do felt like a chore, and I play games to have fun rather than have my patience tested.

@SP

“Wow if you completed this without lookups when English isn’t your first language I bow to you, you are a stud.”

Yeah, I heartily second this.

@Andrzej recently remarked that the forum seemed to have gotten over the novelty of having a Polish participant.

It occurred to me that some of what he was picking up on there might have been a response to the progressive revelation of his personhood, as a godless, poetry-hating, American football-hating, John Kugelman-hating attorney/professor at law with a dog named (after) Satan.

The @Andrzej appeal has perhaps become more selective over time 😉

(btw I’m with Andrzej on everything above, except hating John Kugelman)

@Anonymous

I must protest!

I don't *hate* American football, poetry, or even John Kugelman! I don't care about the first, I don't get the second, and I hate the third's *puzzles* only (admittedly, nothing I know about this constructor indicates we would get along in real life, but that is very much not the same as hate, and since we will never meet, it is immaterial, anyway).

I get what you're saying though. In reality people either like me or can't stand me, and few seem to be neutral about me. I guess that's what you get when you know what you like and want, and don't beat about the bush about anything.

@SP

Yeah, stealth archer was my choice, too - I tend to go for that archetype in most games.

I can't stand the concept of games being hard for hardness's sake. When I hear about a game being all about "punishing challenges" I just cringe. *Why* would I want that in my leisure time? I accidentally tried a soulslike game recently and after 5 hours I was so fed up with it I felt like I might scream. I deleted it, regretting spending the equivalent of a mediocre pizza on it.

If I really love a game, I'll try to beat it on the highest difficulty, after doing it on a lower one first. Thus, I have trophies for completing all of the Uncharted games on the highest difficulty.

@Andrzej

I very much agree, I don't go for things that are hard for the sake of being hard. I have no compunctions over using mods to make a game fun. With KCDII I'm actually enjoying all the running around and the detail in the crafting systems. I'm using poison to make combat more doable. But absolutely to each their own!

@Andrzej

Quoting self from earlier message:

> (btw I’m with Andrzej on everything above, except hating John Kugelman)

Well, I don’t actually *have* a dog that’s named after Satan (both of mine have very conventional names, of the “human-grade” variety).

But I am with you in the sense that I’d be perfectly happy/proud to own a dog named Lucifer*.

*spelling it there as though it was my own little devil beast

@Anonymous

We usually speak of Lucyfer as Lucek, which is a diminutive of the name Lucjan, the English Lucian. Of course, we use a trillion other variants, too, including Lucław and Lucjusz.

@Anonymous
you also have a dog named Satan? ;)

@Bobby 34

Well, he’s a co-editor, so if any clues were changed then it was a cooperative effort.

@Amy

One of my favorite movies—based on short stories by Sherman Alexie, “Smoke Signals”—features radio station KREZ.

@Amy

We were on the same wavelength today. I had RUSSIA before CRIMEA, figured out what came after PR, and originally had RES until I figured out STYLI(Z)E. 🤦‍♂️😄
@Amy I had LATVIA and LEAVE also.  Geography is not my strong suit.  Eventually had to look up CRIMEA and then that corner finally came together.  Good challenge for a rainy Saturday!

@TJ

Not only am I a "nonprofessional solver" (whatever that means), but I'm also Polish, and yet I did this without lookups. Please don't speak for anybody but yourself.

@TJ
I just finished my Comment (which contains--I hope--several well-placed banderillos)...and I disagree with you most heartily.  

I do not know ANYone who solves "professionally"....and I don't know of any "professional competitions" either.  These are open to all.  (Well, as long as you renew annually.)

Best wishes in your future life.

@TJ

I'm pretty new to solving the whole week, so I had to look a couple things up to get toe holds. But this was a great puzzle and I loved it! How would I get better if not challenged?

@TJ I had to look up a few things, but isn't that how we learn?  Took me longer than some other Saturdays, but it was a fun challenge as the "out of the box" clues fell into place.  Only missed the wifi one, as I don't take plane trips.  As for Sam, it is refreshing to see a young person using his intelligence to provide enjoyment to others.  Kudos, Sam!  Some of those Bee words are doozies!!
@TJ so, basically only you.

@Jake G

TJ’s probably long gone.

@TJ  It’s a Saturday puzzle…meant to be challenging. For a long time they seemed nearly impossible for me, but over time I got better and now they are difficult…but usually doable. If you stick with it, you’ll get there without having to turn pro.

@TJ - I'm a nonprofessional, doing puzzles strictly for enjoyment, and finished without any hints or look-ups in just under my average Saturday time. Saturdays are supposed to be the toughest day of the week. I found this puzzle nice and crunchy, and would like more of them.

@TJ 'Non-professional' solver here. Please publish more puzzles by Ezersky and other creators of his calibre. I don't mind having my brain stretched once in a while. The easy puzzles may be good for my streak but the hard ones are good for my soul.

@TJ
Come on bro. 
It’s Saturday!
This is what we all want (well, most of us). 
This took me almost an hour. So I’m probably not a professional. But zero complaints from me.
@TJ  Instead of saying “this one was too hard for me” you’re like “this is an injustice!!” I love it. 10/10.

@TJ,

Well, this got me to thinking. I’ve been doing these puzzles for decades, I have a years-long streak going, I just went to my first ACPT and acquitted myself nicely …

I’m ready to turn pro. 

Where do I sign up?

@TJ I’m no pro. I started solving during the pandemic lockdown and have continued daily. It’s taken six years of solving including extra solving in the archive, but i can now solve puzzles throughout the entire week including this one without lookups. That took sustained effort and lots of errors and I’m still slow - today took 1:18:55 - but the feeling of accomplishment each “hard” day is much more rewarding than the easier ones. As a result, I look forward to late week puzzles and especially ones from the likes of Sam Ezersky.

I remembered that they pre-empted Jeopardy for the NFL Draft last year, and so on Thursday night, I was all set for it not to be on. When it was actually on, I said to myself, great, they’re not doing that this year. 

And then they did it last night. 

Man, I like football, but who gets picked in the draft is something I can read in the paper the next day. And I wouldn’t recognize any of the names (except Mendoza) anyway.

@Bob T.

NOICE, Grant. They are indeed seven-letter songs that precede the rumble (if not immediately), even if the crosses are rough.

@Mean Old Lady and @Grant
we've found overlap! I doubt you'll see this, but I marched down Bway in the Macy's Thanksgiving Day parade playing Mambo in 1977.

@Bob T.
But to those who've seen the original film or most staged versions, the Tonight Quintet is an unquestionable highlight as you hear the romantic leads Tony and Maria (duet), the Jets (chorus), and Anita (solo) each use the same motif to express their anticipation and longing, all coming together in one glorious tutti before the rumble.

@Rome

People love the harder puzzles once they get so good at the easier ones that they need more of a challenge. I started solving before the weekly progression was instituted, so for me, it was just sail through one, then be utterly flummoxed by the next one. 

I suggest you do early-week Archive puzzles and develop better crossword instincts. Also, having an open mind about areas not in your sphere of interest helps. Keep abreast of all current names and words by reading the rest of the paper (or its equivalent). 

Good luck in your future solving!
